pageDescription: Estimate your Social Security benefits based on your earnings history and planned retirement age for comprehensive retirement planning

fullRetirementAge.title: Full Retirement Age
fullRetirementAge.description: Age at which you receive full Social Security benefits (66-67 for most people)

earlyRetirement.title: Early Retirement Impact
earlyRetirement.description: Benefits reduced by 6.67% per year for early retirement before full retirement age

delayedRetirement.title: Delayed Retirement Credits
delayedRetirement.description: Benefits increase by 8% per year for delayed retirement up to age 70

criticalWarningsList.notDiagnostic: This calculator provides Social Security benefit estimates only
criticalWarningsList.actual: Actual benefits may vary based on earnings history and Social Security Administration calculations

importantNotesList: Use your Social Security statement for more accurate benefit estimates
Consider spousal benefits and survivor benefits in retirement planning
Social Security replaces about 40% of pre-retirement income for average earners
Benefits may be subject to federal and state income taxes

messages.enterInformation: Enter your Social Security information to estimate benefits
messages.resultMessage: Your Social Security benefit calculation results are shown below

disclaimer.title: Important Disclaimer
disclaimer.content: This calculator provides Social Security benefit estimates based on general formulas. Actual benefits may vary based on earnings history, Social Security Administration calculations, and other factors not included in these estimates.

seekHelp.title: Financial Resources
seekHelp.content: For personalized Social Security benefit analysis and retirement planning, consult Social Security representatives or financial advisors with expertise in retirement income planning and individual circumstances.

tooltips.dateOfBirth: Your date of birth affects full retirement age calculation
tooltips.retirementAge: Age when you plan to begin receiving Social Security benefits
tooltips.yearsOfCoverage: Number of years you've worked and paid Social Security taxes
tooltips.averageIndexedMonthlyEarnings: Average of your highest 35 years of indexed earnings
tooltips.primaryInsuranceAmount: Benefit amount at full retirement age
tooltips.spouseBenefit: Percentage of your benefit that spouse may receive

show_in_future_inflated_dollars_vs_today: Show in Future Inflated Dollars (vs Today's Dollars)
delayed_retirement_credits_add_8_per_yea: • Delayed retirement credits add 8% per year after FRA
select_claiming_ages_and_calculate_to_se: Select claiming ages and calculate to see your results
your_pia_is_calculated_from_your_average: Your PIA is calculated from your Average Indexed Monthly Earnings (AIME) using bend points:
benefits_are_reduced_for_early_claiming_: Benefits are reduced for early claiming (before FRA) and increased for delayed claiming (after FRA up to age 70).
where_r_investment_return_rate_accountin: Where r = investment return rate, accounting for the opportunity cost of claiming later.
full_retirement_age_is_when_you_can_clai: Full Retirement Age is when you can claim 100% of your Social Security benefit. It ranges from 65 to 67 depending on your birth year. For those born in 1960 or later, FRA is 67.
how_much_do_i_lose_by_claiming_early_at_: How much do I lose by claiming early at 62?
claiming_at_62_reduces_your_benefit_by_a: Claiming at 62 reduces your benefit by about 25-30% permanently, depending on your FRA. The reduction is 5/9 of 1% for each month before FRA (up to 36 months), then 5/12 of 1% for each additional month.
for_each_year_you_delay_claiming_past_fr: For each year you delay claiming past FRA (up to age 70), you earn delayed retirement credits worth 8% per year. This means waiting until 70 can increase your benefit by 24-32% compared to claiming at FRA.
the_breakeven_age_is_when_the_total_bene: The breakeven age is when the total benefits received from claiming later equals those from claiming earlier. Generally, if you live past your early 80s, delaying benefits pays off in total dollars received.
how_does_cola_affect_my_benefits_53: How does COLA affect my benefits?
costofliving_adjustments_cola_are_applie: Cost-of-Living Adjustments (COLA) are applied annually to protect against inflation. The adjustment applies to your benefit amount at the time of the increase, regardless of when you claimed. Historical COLA averages about 2-3% annually.
